revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shinto architecture]] *[[Shinto shrine]]
==References== <references/>
{{Japanese architectural elements}}
[[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Christian architecture]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Garden features]] [[Category:Types of gates]] [[Category:Christianity in Japan]] [[Category:Gates in Japan]] [[Category:Torii]]
{{architecturalelement-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shinto architecture]] *[[Shinto shrine]]
==References== <references/>

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shinto architecture]] *[[Shinto shrine]]
==References== <references/>

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shinto architecture]] *[[Shinto shrine]]
==References== <references/>

Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shinto architecture]] *[[Shinto shrine]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:NAMESPACE}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shinto architecture]] *[[Shinto shrine]]
==References== <references/>
{{Japanese architectural elements}}

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==

This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shinto architecture]] *[[Shinto shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shinto architecture]] *[[Shinto shrine]]

JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>

The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

{{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category: 5]] [[Category:11 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese architectural features]] [[Category:Christian architecture]] [[Category:Shinto architecture]] [[Category:Garden features]] [[Category:Types of gates]] [[Category:Christianity in Japan]] [[Category:Gates in Japan]] [[Category:Torii]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Mihashira Torii]] [[en:Mihashira Torii]]

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[Shinto architecture]] *[[Shinto shrine]]

It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[faith]], [[hope]], and [[charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[:en:Christians|Christians]] to represent the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[:en:Christianity in Japan|Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[:en:Faith|Faith]], [[hope]], and [[:en:Charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[:en:God|God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[Hokusai]] </gallery>

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Mihashira torii}} [[fr:Mihashira torii]] [[ja:三柱鳥居]] [[pt:Mihashira Torii]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[:en:Yamato, Gifu|Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[:en:Christians|Christians]] to represent the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[:en:Christianity in Japan|Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[:en:Faith|Faith]], [[:en:Hope|Hope]], and [[:en:Charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[:en:God|God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[:en:Stream|Stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[:en:Hokusai|Hokusai]] </gallery>

==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[:en:Christianity in Japan|Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[:en:Faith|Faith]], [[:en:Hope|Hope]], and [[:en:Charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[:en:God|God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[:en:Stream|Stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[:en:Hokusai|Hokusai]] </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mihashira torii]][[en:Mihashira Torii]][[fr:Mihashira torii]][[ja:三柱鳥居]][[pt:Mihashira Torii]][[simple:Mihashira Torii]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3532297]] Wikidata name: mihashira torii [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[:en:Yamato, Gifu|Yamato, Gifu]]]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto architecture]]. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[:en:Christians|Christians]] to represent the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[:en:Christianity in Japan|Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[:en:Faith|Faith]], [[:en:Hope|Hope]], and [[:en:Charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[:en:God|God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[:en:Stream|Stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[:en:Hokusai|Hokusai]] </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:Shinto architecture|Shinto architecture]] *[[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]

Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese [[:en:Christians|Christians]] to represent the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[:en:Christianity in Japan|Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[:en:Faith|Faith]], [[:en:Hope|Hope]], and [[:en:Charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[:en:God|God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[:en:Stream|Stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|[[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]] Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by [[:en:Hokusai|Hokusai]] </gallery>

==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early [[:en:Christianity in Japan|Christianity in Japan]].
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes [[:en:Faith|Faith]], [[:en:Hope|Hope]], and [[:en:Charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a [[:en:God|God]].<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a [[:en:Stream|Stream]]. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mihashira torii]][[en:Mihashira Torii]][[fr:Mihashira torii]][[ja:三柱鳥居]][[pt:Mihashira Torii]][[simple:Mihashira Torii]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Mihashira torii]][[en:Mihashira Torii]][[fr:Mihashira torii]][[ja:三柱鳥居]][[pt:Mihashira Torii]][[simple:Mihashira Torii]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Mihashira torii]][[en:Mihashira Torii]][[fr:Mihashira torii]][[ja:三柱鳥居]][[pt:Mihashira Torii]][[simple:Mihashira Torii]]
{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at {{ill|Yamato, Gifu|en|Yamato, Gifu|WD=Q11433541|lt=Yamato, Gifu}}]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture}}. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese {{ill|Christians|en|Christians|WD=Q106039|lt=Christians}} to represent the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early {{ill|Christianity in Japan|en|Christianity in Japan|WD=Q427945|lt=Christianity in Japan}}.
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|WD=Q5410500|lt=Faith}}, {{ill|Hope|en|Hope|WD=Q190507|lt=Hope}}, and [[:en:Charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a {{ill|God|en|God|WD=Q190|lt=God}}.<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a {{ill|Stream|en|Stream|WD=Q47521|lt=Stream}}. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|{{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara}} Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai}} </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture}} *{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}

{{Short description|Shinto torii gate}} {{italic title}} [[Image:Yamato mihasira006.jpg|thumb|''Mihashira Torii'' at {{ill|Yamato, Gifu|en|Yamato, Gifu|WD=Q11433541|lt=Yamato, Gifu|1=Yamato, Gifu}}]] {{nihongo|'''''Mihashira Torii''''' or '''''Mitsubashira Torii'''''|三柱鳥居|Three-pillar Torii| also 三角鳥居 ''sankaku torii''}} are a type of ''[[torii]]'' gate found in {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}}. Like its name implies, it is a triangular structure that appears to be formed from three individual ''torii''. It is thought by some to have been built by early Japanese {{ill|Christians|en|Christians|WD=Q106039|lt=Christians|1=Christians}} to represent the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS>"[http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/m/mihashiratorii.htm mihashira torii 三柱鳥居]." JAANUS. Retrieved on May 31, 2009.</ref>
==History== [[Image:蚕の社三柱鳥居.jpg|thumb|left|280px|''Mihashira Torii'' at [[Konoshima Shrine]]]] While the origins of the ''mihashira torii'' cannot be directly proven, it is sometimes thought to be linked to early {{ill|Christianity in Japan|en|Christianity in Japan|WD=Q427945|lt=Christianity in Japan|1=Christianity in Japan}}.
The primary historical example of a ''mihashira torii'' is found in the records of [[Konoshima Shrine]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. These records states that a triangular ''torii'' was rebuilt in 1716–1736, after a fire. The text hints at a reference to Christianity in the construction of the ''torii'', and says that the three pillars represent the heavens, the earth, and mankind. This is not an interpretation common in [[Shinto]] beliefs. The shrine record also holds that the ''torii'' symbolizes {{ill|Faith|en|Faith|WD=Q5410500|lt=Faith|1=Faith}}, {{ill|Hope|en|Hope|WD=Q190507|lt=Hope|1=Hope}}, and [[:en:Charity (virtue)|charity]]. The Konoshima ''torii'' stands in a pond with a pile of stones in the middle of the structure, supposedly the seat of a {{ill|God|en|God|WD=Q190|lt=God|1=God}}.<ref name=JAANUS />
A private garden in Kyoto also exhibits a ''mihashira torii'', built in a {{ill|Stream|en|Stream|WD=Q47521|lt=Stream|1=Stream}}. The Christian owners of the estate claim to be descended from a family of Christians who practiced their religion in secret during a time when Christianity was strictly regulated in Japan. According to them, this type of ''torii'' was used as a symbol for underground Christians, and that it represented the [[:en:Holy Trinity|Holy Trinity]].<ref name=JAANUS />
==Images==
<gallery> Image:MimeguriMihasira002.jpg|[[Mimeguri Shrine]] Image:Mihasira99144.jpg|{{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|WD=Q127026|lt=Sakurai, Nara|1=Sakurai, Nara}} Image:Mihashiratorii.jpg|A drawing by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|WD=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}} </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shinto architecture|1=Shinto architecture}} *{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}
